figures.caption: Figure 2. ACC evaluation for CIL across 10 tasks on each dataset. Each point represents the average classification accuracy evaluated after learning a given task. For example, the value at task 5 corresponds to the average accuracy of the model on the test sets of tasks 1 through 5 after completing training on task 5. For details, see Fig. 8. Solid lines: buffer-free; dashed lines: memory-based.
Table 4. Performance comparison on CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet across 10 and 20 tasks for CIL scenario. Best results are highlighted for buffer-free and memory-based.
Figure 8. ACC matrix for SNV for the CIL for 10 tasks for each dataset.

Figure 1. An illustration of Shapley Neuron Values where for each task task t − 1, task t, and task t + 1, we identify and freeze the Neurons whose Shapley Values fall within the top r%. Neurons marked with specific colors indicate that the same Neuron appears within the top r% for t specific tasks. τi is each task’s top r% threshold.

Figure 3. Network parameter usage efficiency across datasets in the CIL scenario for 10 tasks. The dashed vertical lines indicate the critical pruning percentage where each method experiences significant accuracy degradation. A sharper decline at lower pruning percentages indicates more efficient usage of network capacity, as it suggests the method utilizes essential parameters with minimal redundancy.

Figure 7. ACC evaluation comparison for the CIL for 20 tasks for each dataset. Each point represents the average classification accuracy evaluated after learning a given task, averaged over all tasks learned up to that point. For example, the value at task 10 corresponds to the average accuracy of the model on the test sets of tasks 1 through 10 after completing training on task 10. For details, see Fig. 9. Solid lines: buffer-free; dashed lines: memory-based.
Table 5. Per-task wall-clock breakdown for SNV. Task 1 requires training only; tasks 2–10 additionally run the TMAB-Shapley valuation phase.
Figure 6. Performance evaluation metrics of continual learning methods. RAC is the Random model ACcuracy.

Table 2. ACC, BWT, and PS performance comparison for TIL scenario on CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet. Best results are highlighted for buffer-free and memory-based. Memory-based methods use a fixed buffer of 200 exemplars.

Table 7. Complete hyperparameter search space for all methods across datasets. All methods use the Adam optimizer with shared parameters β1 ∈ {0.9}, β2 ∈ {0.999}, ϵ ∈ {10−8}. For CIL, memory-based methods use a buffer of 2,000 (CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet) or 20,000 (ImageNet-1k). For TIL, the buffer is fixed at 200.
Figure 11. Mask overlap between tasks across datasets for 10 tasks in the CIL scenario.
Figure 10. Layer-wise Shapley Neuron Importance Across Datasets.
Figure 5. Computational cost of all compared methods across CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet, and ImageNet-1k (Class-IL, 10 tasks). Left: total training FLOPs on a log scale. Right: peak GPU memory.
Table 8. Selected best hyperparameters for all methods across datasets.
Figure 9. ACC matrix for SNV for the CIL for 20 tasks for each dataset.
